slides.title: ECE 20875
slides.content:
Python for Data Science
higher order functions:
filters, map/reduce, list
comprehensions
Qiang Qiu, Murat Kocaoglu, and Anuran Makur
(Adapted from material developed by Profs. Milind Kulkarni,
Stanley Chan, Chris Brinton, David Inouye, and Qiang Qiu)

slides.title: • Since functions are treated as first-
slides.content:
class objects in Python, they can
• Take one or more functions as
arguments
higher order functions
• Return one or more functions

slides.title: • Applies a function to all items in an
slides.content:
input list (i.e., defines a mapping)
• Needs two inputs:
• Function to apply
• Iterable: A sequence, collection, or
iterator object

slides.title: • Perform computation on a list and
slides.content:
return the (single value) result
• Rolling computation applied to
sequential pairs of values
• Needs two inputs:
• Function to apply
• Sequence to iterate over
reduce
• Operator functions can also be
used

slides.title: • Simple way of creating a list based on an
slides.content:
iterable Python object
• Elements in the new list are conditionally
included and transformed as needed
[output expression for item in iterable
if condition]
An example:
• Compared with a for loop
• More computationally efficient
• But less flexible!
list comprehensions
(often better than using map/filter directly)
• Can also have an if-else clause on the output
expression
[output
expression
if-else
clause
for
item
in iterable condition(s) on iterable]
• Can use line breaks between brackets for
readability
• Can also be nested
